1:
Christ turns from silencing his adversaries to instructing "the multitude, and to his disciples," and the witnesses see in this address the necessary distinction between an office and the men who fill it. The Latin fathers press the point that bad teachers do not nullify good doctrine. The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary states the principle: "not the seat makes the Priest, but the Priest the seat... A wicked Priest derives guilt and not honour from his Priesthood," yet "as gold is picked out of the dross, hearers may take doctrine and leave practice, for good doctrine oft comes from an evil man." The Alexandrian Origen distinguishes those who keep "the letter of the Law" (scribes) from those who "separate themselves... as better than they" (Pharisees), and notes that Christ first "puts away from Himself this suspicion" of coveting their place "before he begins to reprove them." The Antiochene Chrysostom adds that "they speak not their own, but God's," and marvels "with how great honour He speaks of Moses, shewing again what harmony there is with the Old Testament."

The puritan-evangelical Henry frames the whole discourse: Christ is "never so severe upon any sort of people as upon these scribes and Pharisees," yet he "allows their office as expositors of the law" while condemning "the men." "Many a good place is filled with bad men," and "good and useful offices... are not therefore to be condemned and abolished, because they fall sometimes into the hands of bad men" — "rather than so, let both grow together until the harvest" (Matthew 13:30). We must not "think the worse of good truths for their being preached by bad ministers; nor of good laws for their being executed by bad magistrates."

The Reformed Calvin gives the design: "amidst the destruction of proper and lawful order, the authority of the word of God might remain entire," lest the people, "offended at the vices of [the scribes], throw away reverence for the Law." Christ "first vindicates the Law of God from contempt," yet "no dread of giving offense prevented Christ from exposing ungodly teachers as they deserved." Ellicott notes the parallel to the Sermon on the Mount: Christ "closes His public teaching, as He began, by a protest against that false casuistry which had substituted the traditions of men for the commandments of God." Wesley adds that Christ now leaves his adversaries "to the hardness of their hearts." The witnesses agree: the coming woes do not overthrow Moses' seat but expose the unworthy who occupy it.
2:
"The scribes and the Pharisees sit in Moses' seat." The witnesses explain the phrase as denoting authority to teach and interpret the Law, and they are largely grammatical here. Barnes: "Moses was a legislator of the Jews. By him the law was given; and the office of explaining that law devolved on the scribes and Pharisees. In the synagogues they sat while expounding the law, and rose when they read it. By sitting in the seat of Moses we are to understand authority to teach the law." Clarke adds that "they sat there formerly by Divine appointment: they sit there now by Divine permission," and that the scribes "are here represented as [Moses'] successors." Robertson notes the "gnomic or timeless aorist," and quotes McNeile: "The heirs of Moses' authority by an unbroken tradition can deliver pronouncements on his teaching."

Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic background — the "seventy-one golden chairs" of the Sanhedrin at Alexandria, the great council as "the pillar and ground of the traditional law" — and corrects those who press the past tense "sat" to mean their power had ceased: rather, "the scribes and Pharisees... have long usurped Moses' seat; nevertheless, we ought to obey them, because, by the dispensation of divine providence, they bear the chief magistracy." Ellicott takes it of "their collective action as represented in the Sanhedrin," recognizing "their official claim to reverence," though perhaps with "a latent protest" like Mark 7:9.

The Reformed Calvin distinguishes the scribes' lawful exposition from their corruptions: Christ "wishes their doctrine to be observed" only "so far as they adhere to the pure and simple exposition of the Law." He answers the Papist appeal to this verse for binding obedience to prelates: Christ also said "Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees" (Matthew 16:6), and "teaching for doctrines the commandments of men" (Matthew 15:9). Hence "we are not bound to embrace, without discrimination, whatever they are pleased to enjoin." He quotes Augustine: "good believers do not obediently listen to any sort of man, but to God himself; therefore we may profitably listen even to those whose lives are not profitable." Cyril of Jerusalem (Eastern), in a catechetical aside, uses "Moses' seat" to prove that David's throne "signifies not his wooden seat, but the authority of his teaching." The witnesses agree: the verse confers authority on the teaching office, not licence on the teachers' corruptions.
3:
"All therefore whatsoever they bid you observe, that observe and do; but do not ye after their works." The witnesses agree the "all" must be qualified, and they fasten on the gap between teaching and practice. Barnes: "all that they teach consistent with the law of Moses... The word all could not be taken without such a restriction, for Christ himself accuses them of teaching many things contrary to that law, and of making it void by traditions" (Matthew 15:1-6). Their interpretation "is in the main correct, but their lives do not correspond." Clarke notes Christ uses a past tense — "whatsoever they have commanded" — and so "may refer to the teaching of a former period, when they taught the way of God in truth," and insists the command cannot be absolute, "because himself warns his disciples against their false teaching."

Ellicott reads the limitation as plain: "so far as they really sit in Moses' seat, and set forth his teaching... they were to be followed with all obedience. That which was wanting was the life." It was "one thing to draw fine pictures of virtue, and another to bring thought and word and deed into conformity with them." Robertson sharpens the distinction: "As teachers they have their place, but beware of following their example... They are only preachers. Jesus does not here disapprove any of their teachings as he does elsewhere. The point made here is that they are only teachers and do not practice what they teach as God sees it." Gill likewise restricts the obedience "to things agreeable to the chair of Moses, to the law of Moses... for otherwise many of their glosses and traditions were repugnant to the law, and ought not to be observed."

Wesley keeps it brief — "all things which they read out of the law, and enforce therefrom." The thinness of the comment here matches the verse's pivot from honouring the office (vv.1-2) to the long indictment of the men that follows. The witnesses converge without dissent: the people are to receive sound doctrine even from corrupt mouths, but never to imitate the corrupt example; truth is not discredited by the hypocrisy of those who teach it, and the deepest charge against the scribes — that "they say, and do not" — is the seed of every woe to come.
4:
"They bind heavy burdens and grievous to be borne, and lay them on men's shoulders; but they themselves will not move them with one of their fingers." The witnesses read this as the taskmaster's cruelty, though they differ on what the "burdens" are. The grammatical-historical Barnes takes them as "the ceremonies and rites appointed by Moses, which Peter says neither our fathers nor we were able to bear" (Acts 15:10) — numerous, costly, laborious — which the Pharisees rigidly required while "indulged themselves, and bore as little of the expense and trouble as possible." Ellicott links it to Christ's own call to the "heavy laden" whose burden he makes "light" (Matthew 11:28-30): "the rigorous precepts, the high-flown morality were for others, not themselves." Robertson sums it: "They are taskmasters, not burden-bearers, not sympathetic helpers." Clarke and Gill, by contrast, take the burdens as "the traditions of the elders," the Pharisees' own additions, "neither reason, expediency, nor revelation to countenance them," with the Gloss calling them traditions "gathered from all sides, not to aid, but to burden the conscience."

The Antiochene Chrysostom marks the "double evil": "they exacted without any allowance the utmost rigour of life from those that were put under them, while they allowed themselves large licence." A good ruler should do the opposite — "to himself a severe judge, to others a merciful one." He notes the force of the words: "He says not they cannot, but they will not; and not, lift them, but touch them with one of their fingers." Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic irony: the "crafty wicked man" was defined as "he that prescribes light things to himself, and heavy to others."

The Reformed Calvin clarifies that Christ does not here charge them with imposing "harsh and unjust laws" (that comes later), but reproves "the stern and rigid manner of teaching" by which "proud hypocrites... authoritatively demand from others what they owe to God, and are rigorous in enforcing duties," while "indolently dispens[ing] with the performance of what they so strictly enjoin." As Ezekiel charged (Ezekiel 34:4), they "rule with sternness and rigour," for "no man will exercise moderation in commanding others, unless he shall first become his own teacher." The witnesses agree: the mark of the false shepherd is rigour toward others and indulgence toward self.
5:
"But all their works they do for to be seen of men." The witnesses identify ostentation — the love of human applause — as the root vice, illustrated by the broad phylacteries and long fringes. The Antiochene Chrysostom notes "the intensive force": not merely some works but "all their works," and "not only in great things but in some things trivial they were vainglorious." The Latin fathers probe the disease: the Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary calls vainglory the worm bred in the wood — "the Devil strives to corrupt the ministry of the Priests... that this good, while it is done to be seen of men, should be turned into evil" — and warns Christians who hang "a portion of the Gospel written out" round the neck: "if in their meaning, they are of more profit when laid up in the heart, than hung round the neck." Jerome explains the literalism: Moses bade them bind his precepts "for a sign upon thine hand" (Deuteronomy 6:8), meaning "let them be fulfilled in thy works"; "this the Pharisees misinterpreting, wrote on parchments the Decalogue... and tied them on their forehead," so "little did they understand that they were to be worn on the heart and not on the body."

The grammatical-historical voices supply the antiquarian detail. Barnes, Lightfoot, Ellicott, and Robertson describe the phylacteries (tephillin) — small leather cases holding four passages (Exodus 13; Deuteronomy 6; Deuteronomy 11), bound on forehead and arm, worn at prayer — and the fringes commanded in Numbers 15:38. The Pharisees "made either the box or the straps wider than the common size" and the fringes longer, "that men might see and admire them." Ellicott traces the word phylacterion to "safeguard or preservative," probably "charms or amulets against the evil eye." Robertson: "The size of the phylacteries indexed the measure of zeal, and the wearing of large ones was apt to take the place of obedience" (Bruce); "such things were useful as reminders; they were fatal when they were regarded as charms."

Ellicott connects the rebuke to Matthew 6:1-18, fixing on "the love of man's applause as that which vitiated the highest ethical teaching." The Reformed Calvin agrees the seeming-good of hypocrites "is hypocritical and worthless, because they have no other design than to please men"; the very aids God gave to keep the Law in remembrance they "turned to luxury and display." The witnesses agree: religion done to be seen has lost its reward before God.
6:
"And love the uppermost rooms at feasts, and the chief seats in the synagogues." The witnesses describe the customs and expose the appetite for precedence. The grammatical-historical Barnes corrects the rendering "rooms": it should be "the uppermost places or couches at feasts," for the Jews reclined rather than sat — "the chief seat... was the middle couch at the upper end of the table. This the Pharisees loved, as a post of honour." The "chief seats in the synagogues" were "the seats usually occupied by the elders... near the pulpit." Ellicott locates these "at the upper or Jerusalem end of the synagogue... where was the ark," given "to those who were most conspicuous for their devotion to the Law, and as such coveted as a mark of religious reputation." Robertson notes the timeless human craving: "an insatiable hunger for prominence" (Bruce), and recalls that "at the last passover meal the apostles had an ugly snarl over this very point of precedence" (Luke 22:24; John 13:2-11), "just two days after this exposure." He adds the wry modern parallel: in Christ's day "the hypocrites boldly sat up in front. Now, if they come to church at all, they take the rear seats."

Gill supplies the rabbinic context — "by the manner of their sitting it was known who was the greatest" — and notes the Scribes and Pharisees "affected" this, "and were pleased if they had it."

The Reformed Calvin draws the spiritual lesson: these signs prove "that no zeal for piety exists in them, but that they are wholly devoted to ambition. For to seek the highest place belongs only to those who choose rather to exalt themselves among men, than to enjoy the approbation of God." He anticipates the coming verses on the title "Rabbi," asserting that "this honour does not belong to any except [Christ]," yet not as a quarrel "about words": Christ "does not attach importance to the title... but restrains them within proper limits, that they may not rule over the faith of brethren," for "his authority must remain entire, and no mortal man ought to claim the smallest portion of it." The witnesses agree: the love of the best seat betrays a heart that prefers human honour to God's.
7:
"And greetings in the markets, and to be called of men, Rabbi, Rabbi." The witnesses describe the public deference the scribes craved and trace the history of the coveted title. Gill pictures them "strolling about the markets... on purpose to be taken notice of before multitudes, with singular marks of respect; as stretching out the hand, uncovering the head, and bowing the knee." Lightfoot supplies the antiquity of the title "Rabbi": "the elder times, which were more worthy, had not need of the title... for, behold, Hillel comes up out of Babylon, and the title of Rabbi is not added to his name"; the title "had its beginning only in the presidents [of the council] from Rabban Gamaliel the old man," near the time of Christ. The honour was thus comparatively new, and the more eagerly grasped for that.

Barnes underscores that the desire "is the love of distinction, the love of titles and of honour." Robertson notes that "Rabbi means 'my great one,' 'my Master,' apparently a comparatively new title in Christ's time." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary (carried from v.5) had unfolded the gradations of the salutation they loved — "first in time, before others; in tone, that we should say with a loud voice, Hail, Rabbi; in body, that we should bow low; and in place, that the salutation should be in public" — concluding "they desire the name, and neglect the duties."

This craving for the title sets up Christ's prohibition in vv.8-10. The witnesses read the love of "Rabbi" as the public face of the same ambition seen in the chief seats (v.6): a hunger to be reverenced as the source and director of others' religion. Ellicott (under v.8) notes that even "the better scribes" had foreshadowings of Christ's teaching — Shemaiah's maxim that "men should love the work, but hate the Rabbi-ship." The thinness of comment here matches the brief verse, but the witnesses agree on its sense: the scribes had made the marketplace and the synagogue stages for the display of a religious eminence that belonged, by right, to God and his Christ alone — and it is precisely this appetite, harmless-seeming in a greeting, that Christ will forbid his disciples to indulge or to gratify in others.
8:
"But be not ye called Rabbi: for one is your Master, and all ye are brethren." The witnesses read this as an apostolic charge against religious self-exaltation. Barnes: "Jesus forbade his disciples to seek such titles of distinction... They were on a level; they were to be equal in authority; they were brethren." The command is "to receive" the title, not to give it where customary; but they were to avoid "everything which would tend to make a distinction among them, or destroy their parity." Barnes presses the application: "This title corresponds with the title Doctor of Divinity... and so far as I can see, the spirit of the Saviour's command is violated by the reception of such a title... It tends to engender pride." Robertson, with Euthymius, softens it: "Do not seek to be called; if others call you this it will not be your fault."

Clarke treats the text: the better reading is "Master" (didaskalos, teacher) rather than "guide," and "even Christ" is likely a later insertion from v.10; he hears an allusion to Isaiah 54:13, "All thy children shall be taught of the Lord." Ellicott notes the foreshadowing in the better scribes — Shemaiah's "love the work, but hate the Rabbi-ship" — and that the disciples "were not to seek the title for themselves as a mark of honour," remembering "Who was teaching them"; the closing words "all ye are brethren" make "the fact that men are disciples of the same Teacher" a "bond of brotherhood."

Wesley draws out what the title implied among the Jews — disciples were required "to believe implicitly what [the rabbi] affirmed, without asking any farther reason; to obey implicitly what he enjoined, without seeking farther authority." Christ, "by forbidding us either to give or receive the title of rabbi, master, or father, forbids us either to receive any such reverence, or to pay any such to any but God." The witnesses agree: the prohibition is not of natural courtesy but of the spiritual lordship that usurps Christ's place over the conscience.

The placing of "all ye are brethren" beside "one is your Master" carries, for the witnesses, the positive truth that the negative command guards. The disciples are not merely forbidden a title; they are constituted a brotherhood, levelled before the one Teacher who alone has the right to bind their faith. Whatever differences of gift or office exist among them — and the New Testament freely names teachers (1 Corinthians 12:28; Ephesians 4:11) — none establishes the kind of mastery the scribes craved, in which the disciple looks to the man rather than to God. James later echoes the same caution, "Be not many masters" (James 3:1). The verse thus does not abolish teaching but purifies it: the true teacher points away from himself to Christ, remembering, as Ellicott notes, "Who was teaching them," and so keeps the whole company brethren under a single heavenly Master.
9:
"And call no man your father upon the earth: for one is your Father, which is in heaven." The witnesses agree the prohibition concerns spiritual authority over the conscience, not natural or civil relations. Gill: Christ "does not mean to set aside all names and titles of natural and civil distinction... but only to reject all such names and titles as are used to signify an authoritative power over men's consciences, in matters of faith and obedience; in which God and Christ are only to be attended to." Barnes: the word "denotes authority, eminence, superiority, a right to command... In this sense it belongs eminently to God, and it is not right to give it to men. Christian brethren are equal." The Jewish teachers "affected that title because they... supposed that a teacher formed the man, or gave him real life."

Clarke connects it to the "Ab, or father of the Sanhedrin," teaching that Christ "would have no Second, after himself, established in his Church." Ellicott traces the parallel history of ecclesiastical titles — "in Abbot (from Abba=Father), in Papa and Pope... in our 'Father in God' as applied to Bishops" — as "examples of the use of like language, liable to the same abuse." But "it would be a slavish literalism to see in our Lord's words an absolute prohibition"; the point is "to warn men against so recognising the fatherhood of men as to forget the Fatherhood of God." Robertson agrees: "the ascription of 'Father' to pope and priest seems out of harmony with what Jesus here says," yet "he should not be understood to be condemning the title to one's real earthly father."

The Reformed Calvin states the principle exactly: "the honour of a father is falsely ascribed to men, when it obscures the glory of God. Now this is done whenever a mortal man, viewed apart from God, is accounted a father; since all the degrees of relationship depend on God alone through Christ." He acknowledges Paul calls himself a spiritual father (1 Corinthians 4:15; Philippians 2:22), so the word is not absolutely forbidden. The witnesses converge: God alone is the source of spiritual life, and no man may be set in his place as the author and governor of another's religion.
10:
"Neither be ye called masters: for one is your Master, even Christ." The witnesses note the deliberate repetition — a third title forbidden, sealing the charge. Gill observes that, though ministers do in a sense guide and direct, "they are to guide them according to the word of God, and not their own dictates... and direct men to a dependence on Christ, as head of the church"; the prohibition is repeated "being a matter of so much importance to the honour of Christ, and men being so apt to set up for masters themselves, in opposition to him, or in conjunction with him, or above him." Barnes distinguishes the word from "Rabbi" in v.8: here it means "leaders... those who go before others; who claim the right to direct and control others," and the command forbids not "proper titles of civil office" (Matthew 22:21; Romans 13:7) but "empty titles, producing distinctions... and claiming that others should acknowledge them to be superior."

Clarke notes the word kathēgētai means "leaders," and that "God is in all these respects jealous of his honour. To him alone it belongs to guide and lead his Church"; Jesus alone "is the sole teacher of righteousness... who can illuminate every created mind." He records Lightfoot's gloss on the threefold title — the legend that King Jehoshaphat would rise and cry "Abbi, Abbi! Rabbi, Rabbi! Mori, Mori! — Father, Father! Rabbi, Rabbi! Master, Master!" — "the three titles which our blessed Lord condemns." Ellicott confirms the word signifies "guide, or leader; the director of conscience rather than the teacher" (Romans 2:19). Robertson adds that kathēgētēs is "the modern Greek word for professor," standing for the more honourable "Rabban."

The Reformed Calvin sees the repetition as confirming "that the lawful order is, that God alone rule over us, and possess the power and authority of a Master; and that Christ subject all to his doctrine," for Christ is "the only head of the whole Church" (Ephesians 1:22), "because the whole body ought to be subject to him and obey him." The witnesses agree: by forbidding three honorific titles in succession — Rabbi, Father, Master — Christ guards his own sole headship over the conscience of his people, leaving to men only the rank of fellow-servants and brethren.
11:
"But he that is greatest among you shall be your servant." The witnesses read this as the great reversal of the kingdom, the antidote to the ambition just condemned. Gill takes it both of the one who "really is greatest, having more grace and greater gifts," and of one who "affected a superiority over others" — perhaps with the disciples' own contention in view — and concludes: "Service is the way to honour; he that would be most esteemed ought to do the most work." Barnes cross-references Matthew 20:26 and pairs it with v.12: "God will exalt or honour him that is humble... That is true religion, and God will reward it."

Ellicott notes the words "admit of a twofold meaning. Either (1)... a law of retribution — the man who seeks to be greatest shall be the servant of all; or (2) they point out the other law, of which our Lord's own life was the highest illustration — that he who is really greatest will show his greatness, not in asserting it, but in a life of ministration." He judges "the latter interpretation seems on the whole to give the best meaning." Clarke takes it as "deacon" (diakonos), service.

The Reformed Calvin draws the conclusion of the whole passage on titles: Christ "did not, after the manner of the sophists, dispute about words, but looked to the fact, that no man, through forgetfulness of his rank, might claim more than was proper. He therefore declares that the highest honour in the Church is not government, but service. Whoever keeps himself within this limit, whatever may be the title which he bears, takes nothing away either from God or from Christ." The Pope, by contrast, "when he is about to oppress wretched souls by tyrannical laws, begins with styling himself servant of servants," but "to insult God openly." Christ "strictly forbids his followers to aspire... any higher than to enjoy brotherly intercourse on an equal footing under the heavenly Father." (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment here is misplaced material on the labourers parable, not this verse.) Wesley simply refers to Matthew 20:26. The witnesses agree: in Christ's kingdom greatness is measured not by rule but by lowly service.
12:
"And whosoever shall exalt himself shall be abased; and he that shall humble himself shall be exalted." The witnesses receive this as a settled law of God's dealings, often repeated by Christ. Gill expounds both halves: the self-exalting man — proud, boastful, coveting titles — "shall lose the honour they have... shall fall into disgrace with men, and are abominable in the sight of God"; while the humble, "entertaining low thoughts of himself... not coveting honour from men," shall "be exalted by God, or men, or both; if not in this world, yet in the world to come." Barnes ties it to Proverbs 15:33 and James 4:6.

Clarke makes it Christological: "Nothing is more hateful in [God's] sight than pride; to bring it into everlasting contempt, God was manifest in the flesh. He who was in the likeness of God took upon him the form of a servant... and humbled himself unto death." He adds a second lesson — "that no man is implicitly to receive the sayings, doctrines, and decisions of any man... in the things which concern the interests of his immortal soul. Christ, his Spirit, and his word, are the only infallible teachers." Ellicott notes the precept "seems to have been one which our Lord desired specially to imprint on the hearts of the disciples. It had been spoken at least twice before" (Luke 14:11; Luke 18:14), with "echoes of it in James 4:10, 1 Peter 5:6." Robertson calls it "characteristic of Christ."

Wesley makes the most of its frequency: "It is observable that no one sentence of our Lord's is so often repeated as this: it occurs, with scarce any variation, at least ten times in the evangelists." The thinness of fresh comment here reflects that the saying was already familiar; the witnesses simply register its force as the divine principle underlying the whole rebuke of Pharisaic ambition. The proud self-exaltation displayed in the love of titles and seats (vv.5-11) stands under a fixed sentence of abasement, while the lowliness Christ requires of his disciples is the sure path to the only exaltation worth having — to be "owned, but crowned, before angels and men," as Henry put it (under v.1). Pride is the reigning sin of the hypocrite, and humility the ornament that is, in God's sight, of great price.
13:
With the first woe the great indictment begins: "ye shut up the kingdom of heaven against men: for ye neither go in yourselves, neither suffer ye them that are entering to go in." The Alexandrian Origen opens with a defence of the very form: the woes, like the curses of the Law, "fall upon the sinner not from Him who denounces, but from themselves who commit the sins," as "a father rebuking a son utters words of cursing, but does not desire that he should become deserving of those curses, but rather that he should turn." He notes the "two commandments... by nature inseparable; because not to suffer others to enter in, is of itself enough to keep the hinderer out." The Pharisees "would neither enter in... nor suffer those to enter in who were able to have believed through... the Law and the Prophets concerning Christ."

The puritan Henry sets the whole structure: "eight woes, in opposition to the eight beatitudes (Matthew 5:3)... gospel curses are of all curses the heaviest," the more dreadful "because of the meekness and gentleness of him that denounced them." "A hypocrite is a stage-player in religion." On this first woe: the scribes "were sworn enemies to the gospel of Christ"; sitting in Moses' seat and pretending to "the key of knowledge," they should have "opened those scriptures of the Old Testament which pointed at the Messiah," showing "Daniel's weeks were expiring, the sceptre was departed from Judah"; instead "they would not go in themselves" (John 7:48) and "would not suffer them that were entering to go in," excommunicating those who confessed Christ. "It is bad to keep away from Christ ourselves, but it is worse to keep others from him."

The grammatical-historical voices keep it concrete. Barnes and Robertson note the "Scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites" formula and the omission of v.14 in the best manuscripts. The Reformed Calvin (carried from v.11) explains the vehemence: Christ thunders "not so much on their account, as for the purpose of withdrawing the common people from their sect... we are compelled at the present day to thunder loudly against the Popish clergy, for no other reason than that those who are tractable may direct their minds to their salvation." Wesley and Gill stress that no man can really shut the gate of heaven that God opens. The witnesses agree: the heaviest charge is to bar others from Christ.
14:
"Ye devour widows' houses, and for a pretence make long prayer." The witnesses brand this the union of greed and feigned devotion, doubly damnable. The Antiochene Chrysostom (twice) marks "the worst": "not from the rich but from widows they took wherewith to fill their bellies, thus burdening the poverty of those whom they should have relieved." And "he that is deriving even the reason for so doing from godliness, and is using this cloke for his wickedness, is justly liable to a far more grievous punishment." The Latin fathers add motive and method: the Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary explains why widows were the prey — a woman "who has her husband to advise her is not so readily deceived" — and instructs Christian ministers not even to give "occasion to suspicions"; "your covetousness you dress up in the colour of religion, and use God's arms in the Devil's service."

The grammatical-historical voices supply the mechanics. Barnes: they "induced widows and poor people to commit the management of their property to them, as guardians and executors, and then took advantage of them," while "by their long prayers they put on the appearance of great sanctity" — prayers "said to have been often three hours in length." Ellicott notes two means — pressing "unjust claims... or becoming their heirs," or "leading devout women, under the show of piety, to bestow on them their estates" — and that "the long prayer refers probably to the well-known Eighteen Prayers." He adds the textual note that "the whole verse is wanting in many MSS., and may have been inserted here from Mark 12:40 or Luke 20:47."

The Reformed Calvin sees here the tearing away of "the disguises of virtues": prayer "contained some evidence of remarkable piety... But Christ says that even the chief part of the worship of God was not used by them without committing sin, because constancy in prayer was with them a trap for base gain." They "sold their prayers... as hirelings dispose of their daily labor"; "the more fervent the appearance... becomes, the more is the name of God profaned." That widows were chiefly imposed on need not surprise, "because silly women are more prone to superstition" (2 Timothy 3:6). The witnesses agree: piety made a tool of plunder earns "the greater damnation."
15:
"Ye compass sea and land to make one proselyte, and when he is made, ye make him twofold more the child of hell than yourselves." The witnesses agree the zeal was real but its fruit corrupt. The Antiochene Chrysostom notes the futility and the harm: "they need so much labour to bring one to salvation; and not only are they slack in conversion, but destroy even those whom they do convert, by corrupting them by example of evil life." The Latin fathers explain the worse condition of the convert: Jerome — before, "he erred in ignorance, and was only a child of hell; but seeing the vices of his masters... he returns to his vomit," "worthy of greater punishment as one that has deserted his cause." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary discerns the motive — "not of compassion... but either from covetousness, that the greater number of worshippers might increase the offerings, or out of vain glory" — and Augustine clarifies that the convert is damned "not by bearing the words of the Law, but by following their doings," "twofold more" because "he neglects to fulfil what he had undertaken of his own choice."

The grammatical-historical voices supply the history and the proverb. Barnes distinguishes "proselytes of righteousness" (fully circumcised converts) from "proselytes of the gate." Clarke reads "compass sea and land" as "you leave no stone unturned," and renders "child of hell" as "an excessively wicked person." Ellicott recalls the forced conversions of John Hyrcanus and the Jewish proverb that "no one should trust a proselyte, even to the twenty-fourth generation" — "proselytes were regarded as the leprosy of Israel." Robertson notes the convert "to Pharisaism rather than Judaism," and the bitter epigram: "the more converted the more perverted."

The Reformed Calvin frames the tragedy: to allure the Gentiles to a degenerate Judaism "was nothing else than to hurry them from Scylla to Charybdis"; the proselytes, "from being lascivious and debauched persons, they render altogether devils," as he charges the monks of his own day, who "are diligent in culling proselytes from every quarter." The witnesses agree: zeal without truth makes the convert worse, not better.
16:
The third woe attacks a casuistry of oaths: "Whosoever shall swear by the temple, it is nothing; but whosoever shall swear by the gold of the temple, he is a debtor." The witnesses expose this as covetousness disguised as scruple. Jerome explains the device: a man who swore "by the temple" and proved false "was not held guilty," but one who swore "by the gold... was immediately compelled to pay" — "and all this they did not out of fear of God, but out of covetousness." Lightfoot connects it to the Corban (Matthew 15:5): swearing by temple or altar was "esteemed as nothing," but swearing "Corban, my gold is for the Temple" was "an indispensable tie." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary draws the Christian parallel: "if one swear by the Gospel, he seems to have done some great thing. To whom we shall say, Ye fools and blind! the Scriptures were written because of God, God is not because of the Scriptures."

The Alexandrian and Latin fathers add the spiritual sense and the logic. Origen reads the temple as "Divine Scripture, the gold the meaning it contains," and the altar as "the human heart." The Gloss states the principle that undoes the distinction: "whoso swears by the creature that is subject, swears by the Divinity that rules over the creation." Hilary: "not Christ by the Law, but the Law by Christ is sanctified."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott links it to Matthew 5:33-37 and traces the casuistry: "the gold of the Temple... had received a more special consecration than the fabric," like the modern scruple that "makes the binding force of an oath depend on kissing the Book." Christ's teaching "sweeps away the arbitrary distinction of different degrees of sanctity," so that "every oath-formula runs up, explicitly or implicitly, into the great thought of God." Robertson notes the third woe omits "hypocrites" and uses "blind guides" (Matthew 15:14), and "he is a debtor" = "is bound by his oath." The Reformed Calvin sees superstition fostered "by the covetousness of pastors": they exalted "the gold and the gift" because it was "a scheme well fitted for collecting prey," so that "the majesty of God and of the temple was undervalued." The witnesses agree: the casuistry served greed and obscured that every oath invokes God himself.
17:
"Ye fools and blind: for whether is greater, the gold, or the temple that sanctifieth the gold?" The witnesses receive Christ's sharp question as the logical demolition of the Pharisees' distinction. Barnes states it plainly: "The gold had no holiness but what it derived from the temple. If in any other place, it would be no more holy than any other gold. It was foolish, then, to suppose that that was more holy than the temple from which it received all the sanctity which it possessed." Gill adds that they "argue after so ridiculous a manner... who must be stupid and sottish to the last degree, and their minds foolishly blinded with avarice."

Robertson addresses the seeming tension with the Sermon on the Mount: "In Matthew 5:22 Jesus had warned against calling a man mōros [fool] in a rage, but here he so terms the blind Pharisees for their stupidity." His conclusion, with McNeile, resolves it: "It shows that not the word but the spirit in which it is uttered is what matters." Christ's "fools" is not the contemptuous insult forbidden in anger, but the just verdict of the Judge upon a wilful and avaricious blindness.

The thinness of comment here matches the brief, pointed verse. The witnesses do not elaborate because the argument is self-evident: the consecrating thing must be greater than what it consecrates. The Pharisees had inverted the order, valuing the lesser (the gold, which profited them) above the greater (the temple, which sanctified it) — and the inversion betrayed that their reverence was governed not by holiness but by gain. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment attached here belongs to the labourers parable, not this verse.) The witnesses agree with the thrust of the whole oath-section (vv.16-22): by their false gradations of sanctity the scribes had not heightened reverence but cheapened it, teaching men to evade the very oaths they swore, and proving themselves "blind guides" who could not see that all holiness in the temple's gold, gift, or fabric flowed from the God whose presence made the place holy at all.

There is a deeper irony the witnesses leave implicit. The scribes prided themselves on knowledge — they were the interpreters of the Law, the guardians of its finest distinctions — yet on the very point where their reasoning was most elaborate, Christ pronounces them "fools and blind." Their cleverness in oath-formulas was the cleverness of men who had lost sight of the one truth that mattered: that nothing is holy of itself, and that all sanctity is derivative, flowing from the God who consecrates. To exalt the consecrated gold above the consecrating temple was not a subtle insight but a gross blindness, betraying that their real reverence was for the gold, which profited them, and not for the God, whose name their oaths invoked. The sharp word "fools" therefore exposes the whole pretension of Pharisaic learning: a knowledge minute in its distinctions yet blind to first things is no wisdom at all, but folly dressed in the robes of the expert.
18:
"And, Whosoever shall swear by the altar, it is nothing; but whosoever sweareth by the gift that is upon it, he is guilty." The witnesses continue the exposure of the oath-casuistry, the comment here being brief and largely grammatical and Reformed. Gill notes these are again "the words or sayings of the scribes and Pharisees, [expressing] their sentiments and practice," for "it was usual with them to swear by the altar; and this was reckoned either no sin at all, or such an oath was not accounted binding." Barnes describes the altar — "the altar of burnt-offerings, in the court of the priests... made of brass, about thirty feet in length and breadth" (2 Chronicles 4:1) — and "the gift that is upon it," the beasts and birds "devoted or given to" God.

The Reformed Calvin supplies the principle that governs the whole section: "our Lord does what ought to be done in correcting errors; for he leads us up to the source, and shows, by the very nature of an oath, that the altar is far more valuable than the gifts which are offered in it." He assumes "that it is not lawful to swear but by the name of God alone," so that "whatever forms men may employ in swearing, they must give to God the honour which is due to him." God "permits himself to be called as a witness and judge by means of such symbols of his presence, provided that he retain his authority unimpaired; for to ascribe any Divinity to the temple would be detestable idolatry." Therefore, "so far as God holds out a brighter mirror of his glory in the altar than in the gift, so much the greater reverence is due to the name of God."

The thinness of fresh comment reflects that the argument simply repeats, for the altar and gift, the logic already pressed for the temple and gold (v.17). The witnesses agree on its sense: the altar, as the place where God's glory is displayed, sanctifies the gift laid on it, and is therefore the greater; to make the lesser gift the more binding object of an oath is both folly and a covetous evasion. The whole device is overturned by tracing every oath back to its only lawful ground — the name and presence of God himself, to which the next verses (vv.20-22) explicitly lead.
19:
"Ye fools and blind: for whether is greater, the gift, or the altar that sanctifieth the gift?" The repeated rebuke draws the briefest comment of the chapter, the witnesses being only Gill and Barnes. Gill observes that the epithet "is very justly repeated, since this is no less an instance of their folly, blindness, and stupidity" — and notes that in some copies and the Vulgate "the word fools is not," though "the Syriac, Arabic, Persic, and Ethiopic versions have it." Barnes states the parallel argument exactly as for v.17: "The altar, dedicated to God, gave all the value or holiness to the offering, and must therefore be the greatest, or of the most importance. If, therefore, either bound to the fulfilment of an oath, it must be the altar" (Exodus 29:37; Exodus 30:29).

The verse is a near-verbatim repetition of v.17, transposed from temple/gold to altar/gift, and the witnesses do not elaborate because the principle is identical: the consecrating thing is always greater than the thing consecrated. The Mosaic law itself had declared that "whatsoever toucheth the altar shall be holy" (Exodus 29:37) — the altar imparting holiness to the gift, not receiving it from the gift. The Pharisees had reversed this plain order, and the reversal was not innocent confusion but a calculated scheme: the "gift" upon the altar passed, in part, to the priests, and so they magnified the obligation attaching to it.

By twice driving home the same logic (vv.17, 19), Christ exposes the whole system of graded oaths as both absurd and corrupt. The thin comment of the witnesses is itself faithful to the text, which needs no expansion: a child can see that the altar which makes the gift holy must be greater than the gift it sanctifies. The lesson, drawn out in the verses that frame it, is that there are no degrees of sanctity by which a man may swear and yet hold himself unbound; every oath, by altar or gift, by temple or gold, reaches up to the God whose presence alone makes any of these holy, and binds the swearer before him.
20:
"Whoso therefore shall swear by the altar, sweareth by it, and by all things thereon." The witnesses read this as Christ's positive correction: every oath, rightly understood, invokes the whole — and ultimately God. Gill is careful to note that "Christ [does not] allow of swearing by the altar, or by the temple, or by heaven, or by any creature... for such swearing is elsewhere disapproved of by him, and forbidden"; but "if a man did swear by the altar, he ought to know and consider that he" swears by all that is on it.

Clarke gives the governing logic: "As an oath always supposes a person who witnesses it, and will punish perjury; therefore, whether they swore by the temple or the gold, or by the altar or the gift laid on it, the oath necessarily supposes the God of the temple, of the altar, and of the gifts, who witnessed the whole, and would, even in their exempt cases, punish the perjury." The supposed loophole was illusory: God stands behind every form.

Wesley draws it out fully: "He that sweareth by the altar, sweareth by it, and by all things thereon — not only by the gift, but by the holy fire, and the sacrifice; and above all, by that God to whom they belong; inasmuch as every oath by a creature is an implicit appeal to God." This is the heart of the matter: there is no such thing as a creature-oath that does not, by implication, call God to witness, for the creature has no sanctity of its own.

The witnesses thus converge with the teaching of the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:33-37): Christ does not endorse swearing by altar or gift, but exposes the dishonesty of supposing that one may swear by them and remain free. The graded distinctions of the scribes were a device for lying under cover of an oath. By insisting that an oath by the altar includes "all things thereon," and (in v.21-22) the temple and heaven include God who dwells there and reigns thence, Christ closes every evasion. The swearer who thinks to bind himself lightly by a lesser object deceives only himself; the God who witnesses all oaths will hold him to every one. Honesty, not casuistry, is what God requires — better, as the Sermon taught, simply "Yea, yea; Nay, nay."
21:
"And whoso shall swear by the temple, sweareth by it, and by him that dwelleth therein." The witnesses press the same logic: to swear by the temple is to swear by God, who made it his dwelling. Barnes identifies "him that dwelleth therein" as God: "The temple was his house, his dwelling. In the first, or Solomon's temple, he dwelt between the cherubims, in the most holy place. He manifested himself there by a visible symbol, in the form of a cloud resting on the mercy-seat" (1 Kings 8:10, 13; Psalms 80:1). Gill notes the popular practice of swearing by the temple "as we have before seen they used to do."

Clarke fastens on a striking textual point. The better manuscripts read not "dwelleth" but "dwelt" (past tense), and he draws out its weight: in the first temple God had "graciously condescended to manifest himself," the Shekinah resting between the cherubim; but the Jews themselves "acknowledge that five things were wanting in the second temple," including the Shekinah. Since "the Lord had long before this time abandoned the Jewish temple, and had now made the human nature of Jesus the Shekinah" (John 1:14, "the Logos... made his tabernacle among us"), Christ "could not, with any propriety, say that the supreme Being did now inhabit the temple," but used a word "that hinted to them that God had forsaken their temple" — anticipating "Behold, your house is left unto you desolate" (v.38). Clarke adds the wider lesson that "all this was common swearing; and, whether the subject was true or false, the oath was unlawful," for "common swearing... has destroyed all respect for an oath."

The witnesses thus complete the argument of the oath-section. The temple has no holiness in its stones; its sanctity is wholly derived from the God who chose to dwell there. To swear by it is therefore to swear by him, and no gradation of formulas can sever the oath from the One it invokes. The scribes' casuistry, which made swearing by the temple "nothing" (v.16), is shown to be not merely illogical but profane — treating as empty the very name of God it implicitly named. And Clarke's textual observation gives the section a solemn undertone: even as Christ corrects their oaths, he hints that the divine presence which alone made the temple holy had already departed, so that the house they swore by was about to be left to them, and to desolation.
22:
"And he that shall swear by heaven, sweareth by the throne of God, and by him that sitteth thereon." The witnesses bring the oath-section to its climax: even heaven, the highest creature-formula, runs up to God himself. Gill notes that "the Jews... in common" swore by heaven but "did not look upon such an oath as obligatory on them" — the very evasion Christ overturns.

Barnes states the conclusion that gathers the whole passage: "Heaven is his throne" (Matthew 5:34). "Jesus says here that all who swear at all, do in fact swear by God, or the oath is good for nothing. To swear by an altar, a gift, or a temple, is of no force, unless it be meant to appeal to God himself. The essential thing in an oath is calling God to witness our sincerity. If a real oath is taken, therefore, God is appealed to. If not, it is foolish and wicked to swear by anything else" (Psalms 11:4; Isaiah 66:1).

The brevity of comment matches the verse, which completes the ascending series begun at v.16: temple → gold, altar → gift, heaven → throne → God. At every step the lesser is shown to derive all its significance from the greater, and the whole chain terminates in God, whose name no oath can escape. The scribes had built an elaborate system of graded obligations precisely to create formulas that sounded binding but were not — a machinery for deceit dressed as reverence. Christ dismantles it by a single principle: there is no creature so distant from God that an oath by it does not invoke him.

The teaching coincides with the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:33-37), where Christ counselled his disciples to swear not at all, but to let their "Yea" be "Yea" and "Nay" be "Nay." Here the same truth is turned against the casuists: if you will swear, know that you swear by God, and tremble to do it falsely. The witnesses agree that the deepest evil exposed in this woe is not merely bad logic but the attempt to use the forms of devotion as instruments of dishonesty — naming God's throne while supposing oneself unbound before the God who sits upon it.
23:
"Ye pay tithe of mint and anise and cummin, and have omitted the weightier matters of the law, judgment, mercy, and faith." The witnesses agree this woe exposes a misplaced scrupulosity — exactness in trifles, neglect of the essential — yet they note Christ does not condemn the tithing. Jerome: the Pharisees "concerned themselves about this alone, that these trifling things should be paid in, but lightly esteemed other things which were weighty"; "the greater of God's commands we swallow and overlook, but shew our carelessness by a religious scrupulousness in little things which bring profit." Origen carefully guards the balance, noting Christ "prudently adds, These ought ye to have done," so that none "might thereupon neglect paying tithes of small things"; the "mint, anise, and cummin" are "the seasoning, not the substantial part," like "abstinence from laughter, fasting, bending the knee," good but secondary to "judgment, mercy, and faith."

The grammatical-historical voices supply the law and the proportion. Barnes details the threefold Jewish tithe (Numbers 18:20-24; Deuteronomy 14:22-29) — "nearly one-third of the property of the Jews was devoted to religious services by law" — and notes the herbs were "of little value," tithing of which "the Pharisees maintained in their extraordinary strictness." Lightfoot confirms "the tithing of herbs is from the Rabbins... added by the scribes, and yet approved of by our Saviour." Ellicott observes the law (Deuteronomy 12:17) named only "corn, wine, and oil," but the Pharisee "made a point of gathering the tenth sprig of every garden herb"; so far as done "at the bidding of an imperfectly illumined conscience our Lord does not blame it. What He did censure was the substitution of the lower for the higher." Robertson: "Jesus does not condemn tithing. What he does condemn is doing it to the neglect of the weightier matters."

The Reformed Calvin anticipates the calumny that Christ "set no value on what the Law had enjoined": rather he "acknowledges that whatever God has enjoined ought to be performed," yet maintains "due order," that "all the commandments are so interwoven... that we have no right to detach one of them from the rest" (Deuteronomy 27:26; Galatians 3:10). Wesley glosses "judgment" as justice and "faith" as fidelity. The witnesses agree: the small duty must be done, but never as a substitute for the great.
24:
"Ye blind guides, which strain at a gnat, and swallow a camel." The witnesses delight in the proverb's vividness and unite in correcting a famous misprint. Barnes states the textual point: "To strain AT a gnat conveys no sense. It should have been, to strain OUT a gnat... The common reading is a misprint." The Greek means "to strain out by a cloth or sieve"; the gnat, in its grub form, lived in water, and "those who were nice in their drink would take pains to strain it out." The proverb sets "a very small matter" against "a large object," and Barnes cites the Arab parallel: "He eats an elephant, and is suffocated with a gnat."

Clarke and Calvin both document the misprint at length. Clarke shows that "Ye blinde gides, which strayne out a gnat" stood in Tyndale (1534), Cranmer, Geneva, and Erasmus's translation (1547), and that "strain at" was "at first an error of the press... which escaped in the edition of 1611, and has been regularly continued since." Calvin's editor lists the same versions, concluding "the substitution of at for out is more likely to have been the effect of accident than of design." Wesley calls it "that glaring false print... which quite alters the sense."

Ellicott and Robertson supply the ceremonial point: both gnat and camel "were ceremonially unclean" (Leviticus 11:4, 23, 42); the devout Jew literally filtered his drink "in accordance with Leviticus," while the camel appears "as the type of vastness" and "among the unclean beasts." Robertson cites the rabbinic saying, "He that kills a flea on the Sabbath is as guilty as if he killed a camel."

The Reformed Calvin draws the meaning: the proverb "beautifully describes the affected scrupulousness of hypocrites about trifling matters; for they utterly shrink from very small faults... and yet they freely permit themselves and others to commit the most heinous crimes." They are "excessively rigid and severe in matters of no great importance," while "they pass by and even tear in pieces the whole Law"; "in this way... they proudly spit in [God's] face." The witnesses agree: the gnat strained and the camel swallowed picture a conscience meticulous in the negligible and reckless in the grave.
25:
The fifth woe turns to inward defilement: "ye make clean the outside of the cup and of the platter, but within they are full of extortion and excess." The witnesses read the cup as a figure of the man, clean without and foul within. Jerome: Christ "means not that their scrupulousness respecting the cup was of any importance, but that they affected it to pass off their sanctity upon men." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary draws the contrast: the Jews washed "themselves, their clothes, and their vessels, but none cleansed himself from his sins"; "this He speaks not of the cup of sense, but of the understanding, which... if it have sinned, though the water of the whole ocean have washed it, is foul and guilty before God." Hilary: "it is the inside of the cup that is used; if that be foul, what profit is it to cleanse the outside?" Origen reads it of teachers who "study to set forth well wrought discourse rather than such as is full of healthful meaning."

The Antiochene Chrysostom notes a difference from the tithing woe: there Christ said "these ought ye to have done"; but "here, discoursing of things clean and unclean, He does not add this, but distinguishes and shews that external purity of necessity follows internal."

The grammatical-historical voices weigh the words. Barnes: "The outside appeared well. The inside was filled with the fruit of extortion, oppression, and wickedness... their cups, neat as they appeared, were filled with the fruits of dishonest industry." Robertson sharpens it: "These punctilious observers of the external ceremonies did not hesitate at robbery (harpages) and graft (akrasias), lack of control... a modern picture of wickedness in high places." Ellicott reads "extortion and excess" as pointing both to "the source from which the viands came" and "that to which they tended — unrestrained self-indulgence." The Reformed Calvin: by "the outside" Christ "metaphorically expresses the outward appearance," reproving their hypocrisy "in not endeavoring to regulate their life, except before the eyes of men." Wesley notes the double censure: "they procured unjustly what they used intemperately." The witnesses agree: it is folly to polish the cup's outside while it brims with the proceeds of plunder.
26:
"Thou blind Pharisee, cleanse first that which is within the cup and platter, that the outside of them may be clean also." The witnesses receive Christ's remedy: purify the heart, and the life follows. Gill notes the aptness of "blind Pharisee" for one "so scrupulously careful to cleanse his cup and platter, and yet made no conscience of filling them with what was gotten in an unjust way." Barnes states the lesson: "Let them be filled with the fruits of honest industry, and then the outside and the inside will be really clean... it was necessary to cleanse the heart first, that the external conduct might be really pure and holy."

Ellicott unfolds the implied premise: "uncleanness in its ethical sense was altogether distinct from the outward uncleanness with which the Pharisees identified it. If the contents of the cup were pure in their source and in their use, they made the outside clean, irrespective of any process of surface purification." The order is decisive — inside first, and the outside takes care of itself; reverse it, and all the scrubbing is in vain. Robertson (under v.25) had noted "the change to singular in verse 26 as if Jesus in a friendlier tone pleads with a Pharisee to mend his ways" — the lone note of entreaty amid the thunder of the woes.

The witnesses thus read this verse as the positive counterpart to the woe of v.25. Christ does not merely expose the hypocrisy of outward purity; he points the way out of it. The defilement that matters is moral, seated in the heart, and it is there that cleansing must begin — exactly the principle he had taught in Matthew 15:11, 18-20, that what defiles a man comes from within. (Wesley's note attached here belongs to v.27, on the tombs of the prophets.) The promise embedded in the command is gracious: a heart made clean will issue in a clean life, "the outside also" becoming pure not by polishing but by the purity within. The single Pharisee addressed in the second person — "Thou blind Pharisee" — is not only condemned but invited; the friendlier singular hints that even these whitewashed men might yet be cleansed if they would let the washing begin where God looks, in the inward part. The witnesses agree: true reformation works from the heart outward, never from the surface inward.
27:
The sixth woe sharpens the image: "ye are like unto whited sepulchres, which indeed appear beautiful outward, but are within full of dead men's bones, and of all uncleanness." The witnesses explain both the custom and its sting. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Lightfoot describe the practice: tombs were "annually whitewashed, to prevent the people from accidentally coming in contact with them" and contracting ceremonial defilement (Numbers 19:16); "thus whited, they appeared beautiful; but within they contained the bones and corrupting bodies of the dead." Lightfoot dates the whitening to "the fifteenth day of the month Adar," so that "uncleanness cries out to you and saith, Come not near." Ellicott notes the participle means "smeared with lime powder," and that the tombs, freshly whitened about March, would be "shining in their new whiteness" at Passover; he distinguishes the parallel in Luke 11:44, which speaks of unmarked graves men tread on unawares. Robertson adds that these were "the sepulchres of the poor in the fields," not the rock-hewn tombs of the rich, and that in Acts 23:3 Paul calls the high priest "a whited wall."

The Latin and Alexandrian fathers draw the moral. Origen: "all feigned righteousness is dead, forasmuch as it is not done for God's sake... no righteousness at all, any more than a dead man is a man, or an actor... is the man whom he represents." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary turns it into a challenge: "if it be good to be wicked, why do you not desire to seem that which you desire to be?... Either be what you seem, or seem what you are." Jerome: "crooked teachers who teach one thing and do another... affect purity in their dress, and humility in their speech, but within are full of all uncleanness, covetousness, and lust." Gregory adds they cannot plead ignorance, "for by assuming every form of sanctity, they witness against themselves that they are not ignorant how to live well."

The Reformed Calvin: hypocrites "deceive by their outward appearance, because they are full of deceit and iniquity," like a marble tomb that "wears the aspect of what is beautiful and lovely, but covers a stinking carcass." The lesson is general: "the children of God ought to desire to be pure rather than to appear so." The witnesses agree: a fair exterior over inward corruption is the very portrait of hypocrisy.
28:
"Even so ye also outwardly appear righteous unto men, but within ye are full of hypocrisy and iniquity." The witnesses receive this as the plain application of the whited-sepulchre figure. Gill lists the outward shows that made them "like whited sepulchres, outwardly beautiful": "making broad their phylacteries, enlarging the borders of their garments, praying long prayers, compassing sea and land to make one proselyte, paying tithes of all manner of herbs, and cleansing the outside of the cup and platter, and doing all their works... to be seen of men" — "they looked like righteous persons, and were not."

Lightfoot supplies a vivid rabbinic parallel: such hypocrites "are called distained, or coloured. Jannai the king, when he was dying, warned his wife that she should take heed of painted men, pretending to be Pharisees, whose works are as the works of Zimri, and yet they expect the reward of Phineas" — "coloured without, but their inward part doth not answer to their outward." He adds the Jewish maxim they had forgotten: "A disciple of the wise, who is not the same within that he is without, is not a disciple of the wise"; and notes drily, "The masters themselves acknowledged this to their own shame." Ellicott cites the same Maccabean saying of Alexander Jannaeus, warning against "men who were painted Pharisees, expecting the reward of Phinehas, while their works were the works of Zimri," and renders the closing word not "iniquity" but "lawlessness — a reckless disregard of the very Law of which they professed to be the interpreters."

Clarke turns it to the reader's conscience and to the final judgment: "what will this appearance avail a man, when God sits in judgment upon his soul? Will the fair reputation which he had acquired among men, while his heart was the seat of unrighteousness, screen him from the stroke of that justice...? No." A man "shall be judged and punished" according to the sin in which he died, and "his profession of holiness only tends to sink him deeper." His closing word gathers the woe: "Reader! see that thy heart be right with God."

The witnesses agree: the fair exterior the Pharisees presented to men was the precise inverse of their inward state, and God, who searches the heart, sees through it. Their own teachers had condemned the "painted" man; their own conscience, and the judgment to come, witnessed against the appearance they so carefully maintained. The lesson of the whole woe is that hypocrisy, however well it deceives men, is laid bare before the One who judges the secrets of the heart.
29:
The last woe: "ye build the tombs of the prophets, and garnish the sepulchres of the righteous." The witnesses read this as a subtle self-condemnation — honouring dead prophets while sharing the murderous spirit of those who slew them. Jerome calls it "a most subtle syllogism" proving "them to be the sons of murderers"; their "ambitious and magnificent structures" proclaim by deed what they deny in word. The Antiochene Chrysostom discerns the design: Christ "does not blame them for building the sepulchres, but discovers the design... which was not to honour the slain, but to erect to themselves a triumphal monument of the murder," lest "the memory of this their audacious wickedness should perish." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary draws the universal lesson: "each readily apprehends the other's fault, but none his own... He only is the truly righteous and wise who is able to judge himself"; the Jews "have ever been adorers of saints of former times, and contemners, yea persecutors, of the living."

The grammatical-historical voices supply the monuments. Barnes notes building tombs "professedly from veneration," common "among the Mohammedans." Ellicott points to "four conspicuous monuments... at the base of the Mount of Olives, in the Valley of Jehoshaphat," of Herodian architecture — possibly "the very sepulchres of which our Lord spoke" — bearing the names Zechariah, Absalom, Jehoshaphat, James; he notes the irony that they "lavished their wealth" on a prophet's tomb "at the very time when they were about to imbrue their hands in the blood of One who was... both a prophet and a righteous man." He quotes the Jerusalem Talmud: "there is no need to adorn the sepulchres of the righteous, for their words are their monuments," and the parallel in Tacitus. Robertson: "These men who professed to be so distressed at the murdering of the Prophets were themselves compassing the death of Him who was far greater than any Prophet."

The Reformed Calvin exposes the hypocrisy with a famous flourish: "it is customary with hypocrites thus to honour, after their death, good teachers... whom they cannot endure while they are alive," for "the ashes of the dead no longer give annoyance by harsh reproofs." He turns it on Rome: "If Peter were now alive, they would tear him in pieces; they would stone Paul; and if Christ himself were still in the world, they would burn him with a slow fire." The witnesses agree: to honour dead prophets while persecuting living ones is hypocrisy's masterpiece.
30:
"And say, If we had been in the days of our fathers, we would not have been partakers with them in the blood of the prophets." The witnesses unmask this disclaimer as unconscious — or calculated — self-deception. Gill paraphrases their boast: "if they had lived in their times, or had been in the same post and office, they should have opposed, at least not consented to" the killing of the prophets. Barnes notes they professed this "by rebuilding their tombs" and "probably expressed their disapprobation of the conduct of their fathers," all "a profession of extraordinary piety" — which "our Lord showed was a mere pretence."

Clarke is blunt: "They imagined themselves much better than their ancestors; but our Lord, who knew what they would do, uncovers their hearts, and shows them that they are about to be more abundantly vile than all who had ever preceded them." Ellicott, more reflective, finds it "an instance of the unconscious hypocrisy of which every generation has more or less been guilty, when it has condemned the wrong-doing of the past... and then has yielded to the same sins itself." He grants "there is no necessity for assuming that the Pharisees did not mean what they said" — the deceit was sincere, which made it the more damning.

The Reformed Calvin discerns Christ's strategy: he "takes a passing glance of their foolish boasting, in being accustomed to glory in their ancestors, while they were descended from the bloody enemies of God." The appeal runs: "You look upon the veneration which you pay to the deceased as some sort of expiation for the wickedness of your fathers. Now then I have this to urge, that it is in vain for you to boast of a sacred ancestry, since you are descended from wicked and ungodly parents... it is an additional and far more heinous crime, that the sacrilegious fury of the fathers... is imitated by you in the murder of the living."

The witnesses agree: the disclaimer convicts the speakers. By distancing themselves from their fathers' crimes while preparing to outdo them, the scribes prove they share the very spirit they condemn. Their fine words — "we would not have been partakers" — are the words of men who do not know their own hearts, and who, within days, would consent to the murder of the Lord of the prophets. The self-righteous condemnation of past sin, joined to the present commission of the same sin, is the subtlest and most self-betraying form of hypocrisy.
31:
"Wherefore ye be witnesses unto yourselves, that ye are the children of them which killed the prophets." The witnesses note the force of "unto yourselves" — their own words convict them. Gill: their disclaimer was "a plain acknowledgment, and a full confession, what their fathers had done, and whose offspring they were; and from whom better things were not to be expected; since they were their fathers' own children, and of the same temper."

Barnes locates the emphasis precisely: "It was not by their building the tombs that they were witnesses... but in spite of all this pretence to piety — under cloak of all this profession — they knew in their consciences, and were witnesses to themselves, that it was mere hypocrisy, and that they really approved the conduct of those who slew the prophets." They were "children of them" not merely by descent but by spirit: "they not only showed that they were descended from them, but that they possessed their spirit, and in similar circumstances would have done as they did" (Acts 7:52; 1 Thessalonians 2:15). Ellicott: "Their words were true in another sense than that in which they had spoken them. They were reproducing in their deeds the very lineaments of those fathers whom they condemned."

Clarke applies it to every age: "There are many who think that, had they lived in the time of our Lord, they would not have acted towards him as the Jews did. But we can scarcely believe that they who reject his Gospel, trample under foot his precepts... love sin, and hate his followers, would have acted otherwise to him than the murdering Jews, had they lived in the same times." Wesley sums it: "ye testify against yourselves, by your smooth words as well as devilish actions, that ye are the genuine sons of them who killed the prophets... while they professed the utmost veneration for those of past ages." He adds a summary of the whole woe-section (vv.3-30), cataloguing what "commonly passes in the world for religion" — punctuality in prayer, zeal for proselytes, reverence for places, exactness in trifles, outward cleansing, a specious face of virtue — "whereby the pretenders to it keep both themselves and others from entering into the kingdom of God."

The witnesses agree: the scribes' own mouths sentence them. By naming themselves the "children" of the prophet-killers, even while disclaiming their fathers' guilt, they confess a kinship of spirit they were about to prove in deed — and their conscience, appealed to by Christ, bears the witness no court could compel.
32:
"Fill ye up then the measure of your fathers." The witnesses read this not as a command but as a prophecy, exposing the scribes' coming crime. The Antiochene Chrysostom: "This is not a command, but a prophecy of what is to be," holding over them "an unspeakable terror" — that "upon you may come all the righteous blood." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary illustrates: "as suppose you had a quarrel with some one, you might say, Do to me what you are about to do; but you do not bid him do it, but shew him that you are aware of his manoeuvres." It notes they "went beyond the measure of their fathers; for they put to death only men, these crucified God." It also unfolds the principle of accumulated judgment: "as all the good things merited by all the saints since the foundation of the world were bestowed on that last generation which received Christ, so all the evil... came upon that last generation of the Jews which rejected Christ."

The Alexandrian Origen: "they fill up the measure of their fathers' sins by their not believing in Christ," because "they looked only to the letter and the body."

The grammatical-historical voices keep it sober. Barnes (carried into the next verses) reads it as the nation filling up its iniquity by slaying the Messiah and his messengers, so that "the patience of God is exhausted." Robertson notes the imperative as ironical, pointing to the deed they are about to do. The Reformed Calvin reads it as Christ exposing their hypocrisy: their veneration of dead prophets is no expiation, and "the sacrilegious fury of the fathers... is imitated by you in the murder of the living."

The witnesses agree on the sense: Christ, with terrible irony, bids them complete what their hearts are already set upon. The "measure" is the cup of national guilt, filling up across the generations until it overflows in the rejection and crucifixion of the Son. The command-form is a prophecy and a sentence: go on, then, and do what you are determined to do — but know that the whole accumulated debt of innocent blood will be required of the generation that fills the cup to the brim. The verse marks the turning point of the chapter, from indictment of sin to the announcement of the judgment (vv.33-36) that the completed measure will inevitably bring.
33:
"Ye serpents, ye generation of vipers, how can ye escape the damnation of hell?" The witnesses hear the climax of the woes — the sternest language Christ ever used. Gill explains the names: serpent and viper "express their craft and subtlety, their inward poison and venomous nature; their fair outside and specious pretences; their hypocrisy, malice, and wickedness," likening them "to the old serpent, their father the devil, and to their ancestors, that murdered the prophets." Lightfoot notes "the damnation of hell" is "the judgment of Gehenna."

The grammatical-historical voices mark the echo of John the Baptist. Ellicott: "the word of rebuke which had come before from the lips of the Baptist (Matthew 3:7), comes now, with even more intense keenness, from those of the Christ." Robertson: "These blistering words come as a climax and remind one of the Baptist and of the time when the Pharisees accused Jesus of being in league with Beelzebub (Matthew 12:34). They cut to the bone like whip-cords." He cites a Talmudic curse: "Woe to the house of Annas! Woe to their serpent-like hissings." Clarke draws out the viper imagery — the ancient belief that the young "eat their way through the womb of their mothers" — applied "with what propriety... to the Jews, who were about to murder the very person who gave them their being."

Barnes adds a crucial caution about the language: "This is the sternest language that Jesus ever used to wicked men. But it by no means authorizes ministers to use such language to sinners now... Christ knew that this was true of them. He had an authority which none now have... He knew the hearts of men; we know them not... We address characters." Christ could declare "certainly that those whom he addressed would be lost"; we have no such authority.

The Reformed Calvin explains the vehemence: Christ, "being about to close his discourse, kindles into more vehement indignation... as it is necessary to shake off by violence the flatteries in which hypocrites indulge, and to drag them to the judgment seat of God." He "intended to strike terror into the whole people, that all might guard against a similar destruction," for these were "the worst of hypocrites... who had captivated the multitude by their enchantments." Wesley: "Our Lord having now lost all hope of reclaiming these, speaks so as to affright others from the like sins." The witnesses agree: the brood of vipers, ripe for Gehenna, are held up as a warning to all.
34:
"Wherefore, behold, I send unto you prophets, and wise men, and scribes: and some of them ye shall kill and crucify." The witnesses note that Christ speaks with divine authority, sending messengers as God had of old, and foreseeing their fate. Gill: Christ "here speaks as one having power and authority, to qualify and send forth men," and "of what he should do after his resurrection: for notwithstanding the people of the Jews would crucify him... yet after all this, he would send his ministers to them, to gather his elect." Lightfoot notes the "wise men and scribes" mark a "distinct calling," learned and "separated from the common people by a distinct order and office."

The puritan Henry marks the strangeness and the mercy of the connection: "You are a generation of vipers, not likely to escape the damnation of hell; and yet I send unto you prophets." Christ "designs yet to try them with the means of grace," sending Christian "prophets, and wise men, and scribes" — the apostles and their successors — even to a people he has just pronounced ripe for ruin. The sending is at once a final overture of grace and the occasion that fills up their measure, for "some of them ye shall kill and crucify, and some of them ye shall scourge in your synagogues, and persecute from city to city."

The grammatical-historical voices read it of the Christian ministry. Barnes and Ellicott see fulfilment in Stephen (Acts 7:60), James (Acts 12:2), and the apostolic persecutions (1 Thessalonians 2:15). The "I send" is the word of the Lord of the prophets, claiming to commission messengers as Wisdom and as God.

The Reformed Calvin reads it as proof of Christ's eternal Godhead: he "speaks here in the person of God," of "the care which he exercised about the salvation of his people from the beginning"; for "the Church was governed by God in such a manner that Christ, as the Eternal Wisdom of God, presided over it." Wesley simply notes that the ministers of the word have "a distinct calling." The witnesses agree: in sending his own messengers to a doomed generation, Christ both extends a last mercy and seals the guilt of those who will reject and slay them, as their fathers slew the prophets before.
35:
