"That upon you may come all the righteous blood shed upon the earth, from the blood of righteous Abel unto the blood of Zacharias son of Barachias, whom ye slew between the temple and the altar." The witnesses wrestle chiefly with the identity of Zacharias and with the justice of visiting the fathers' blood on this generation. On the identity, the dominant view is that of Lightfoot, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson, and Calvin: the reference is to Zechariah son of Jehoiada, stoned "in the court of the house of the Lord" by order of King Joash (2 Chronicles 24:20-22), the last martyr in the Hebrew canon's order (Chronicles closing the Jewish Bible), so that "from Abel to Zechariah" spans the whole of Scripture's martyrs. The difficulty that Matthew names him "son of Barachias" (whereas Chronicles says Jehoiada) is met variously: Clarke and Ellicott note the names mean the same ("blessing of Jehovah"), that Jerome found "Jehoiada" in the Gospel of the Nazarenes, and that double names were common; Ellicott and Robertson list it among possible scribal harmonizations, noting the Sinaitic MS omits "son of Barachiah." Ellicott records the Talmudic legend that Zechariah's blood "would not dry up" until Nebuzaradan took the temple, and that Josephus reports a later "Zechariah son of Baruch" slain in the temple by the Zealots — names that may have "linked the two together in men's minds."

On the justice, the grammatical-historical Barnes explains: "God did not hold them guilty for what their fathers did; but temporal judgments descend on children in consequence of the wickedness of parents," as a drunken father impoverishes his children; "the appropriate effects of their fathers' crimes were coming on the nation." The Reformed Calvin grounds it in shared guilt: "since they are all involved in a wicked conspiracy... God, in punishing all without reserve, makes the punishment due to the fathers fall upon the children"; the whole nation, "in whatever age," is justly held guilty of "unceasing contempt." He explains why Abel and Zechariah are named (not the later martyrs under Manasseh): Christ "selects this murder, which was more ancient — the commencement and source of base licentiousness," and Abel as the first, with Cain as "the head and leader" whose imitators they were. Wesley notes the place — "between the temple [the inner sanctuary] and the altar [in the outer court]." The witnesses agree: the blood of every righteous martyr, from the first to the last, would be required of the generation that crowned the long rebellion by killing the Lord of the prophets.
36:
"Verily I say unto you, All these things shall come upon this generation." The witnesses read this as the solemn seal of the woes — the judgment is near and certain. Barnes: "The destruction of Jerusalem took place about forty years after this was spoken." Clarke renders "this generation" as "this race of men, viz. the Jews," noting the phrase recurs in that sense throughout the evangelists. Gill calls "Verily I say unto you" the usual form "to raise attention... and to ascertain the truth of it."

Ellicott unfolds the principle of accumulated and ratified guilt: "Men make the guilt of past ages their own, reproduce its atrocities, identify themselves with it; and so, what seems at first an arbitrary decree, visiting on the children the sins of the fathers, becomes in such cases a righteous judgment. If they repent, they cut off the terrible entail of sin and punishment; but if they harden themselves in their evil, they inherit the delayed punishment of their fathers' sins as well as of their own." The judgment is not blind fate but the just harvest of a settled rebellion that this generation makes its own by completing it.

The brevity of comment matches the verse, which simply fixes the time of the reckoning: "this generation" — the very men Christ addressed, within whose lifetime the temple and city would fall (the theme taken up in Matthew 24). The accumulated blood of all the prophets, traced from Abel to Zechariah (v.35), would be required not of some distant posterity but of the contemporaries who filled the cup by rejecting and crucifying the Messiah and persecuting his messengers.

The witnesses agree on the verse's force: it transforms the woes from rebuke into prophecy. What the scribes were and did would issue, within forty years, in the destruction of the very house and nation they trusted. (The Cyril of Jerusalem fragment attached here is a catechetical aside on the Holy Spirit, unrelated to the verse.) The door of repentance stood open even now — Ellicott's "if they repent, they cut off the entail" — but the settled hardness of the generation made the judgment, humanly speaking, sure; and Christ, having pronounced it, turns in the next verses from threat to lament over the city he still longed to gather.
37:
"O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, thou that killest the prophets... how often would I have gathered thy children together, even as a hen gathereth her chickens under her wings, and ye would not!" The witnesses hear, after the thunder of the woes, a cry of grieved love. The Antiochene Chrysostom: "this repetition of the name is a mark of compassion and intense love... Notwithstanding these thy murders have not alienated Me from thee, but I would have taken thee to Me, not once or twice, but many times." Augustine probes the mystery of the unfulfilled will: "Was it not that she would not that her children should be gathered by Him, and yet He did, notwithstanding, gather those of her children whom He would?" Origen explains "how often," though Christ taught there bodily but once, "because Christ was ever present in Moses, and in the Prophets... in every generation."

The grammatical-historical voices note the tenderness and the freedom. Robertson: "how often did I long to gather to myself," the very verb used "of the hen with the compound preposition"; the words "naturally suggest previous visits to Jerusalem made plain by John's Gospel." Ellicott observes the lament "had been uttered once before" (Luke 13:34-35), and was surely present when Christ "wept over" the city (Luke 19:41); the present tense "thou that killest" embraces past and future, foreseeing the deaths of Stephen and James. On "ye would not," he writes: "No words could more emphatically state man's fatal gift of freedom, as shown in the power of his will to frustrate the love and pity, and therefore the will, even of the Almighty." Clarke draws the same: Christ "did every thing that could be done... to effect [their salvation]... these persons nevertheless perished... because they would not come unto him" (John 5:40).

The Reformed Calvin reads it as "indignation rather than compassion" toward the scribes, yet genuine pity for the city. He marvels at the condescension: "When he compares himself to a mother, he descends very far below his glory; how much more when he takes the form of a hen." On the long-debated "ye would not," he distinguishes God's revealed will from his secret counsel: "the will of God which is here mentioned must be judged from the result. For since by his word he calls all men indiscriminately to salvation... it may justly be said that he willed to gather all to himself." It is "not the secret purpose of God, but his will which is manifested by the nature of the word, that is here described." Against those who use the text "to set aside the secret predestination of God," he replies that Scripture often ascribes human feelings to God in accommodation to our weakness. The witnesses agree: here is divine love spurned by human will, the Saviour weeping over the city that would not be saved.
38:
"Behold, your house is left unto you desolate." The witnesses read this as the sentence of abandonment — the temple forsaken by God and doomed to ruin. Gill: "the city... would, in a little time, within the space of forty years, be destroyed, and become a desert; and the temple, formerly the house of God, but now only their's... would be abandoned by God," and "the Messiah, the proprietor of it, and who was now in it, would then take his leave of it, and never more return." Barnes: "Your house. The temple... About to be desolate, or destroyed. To be forsaken as a place of worship, and delivered into the hands of the Romans" (Matthew 24:1).

Clarke fastens on the pronoun: "It was once the Lord's temple, God's own house; but now he says, Your temple or house — to intimate that God had abandoned it" (recalling his note on v.21, that God "dwelt" there in the past tense). Ellicott notes the word "desolate" is omitted in some of the best manuscripts, and that "your house" may mean "the whole polity of Israel" or "more specifically the Temple, which was the joy of their hearts. It had been the house of God, but He, as represented by His Son, was now leaving it for ever. It was their house now, not His" — spoken "as our Lord was departing from the Temple (Matthew 24:1), never to reappear there."

The Reformed Calvin exposes the false confidence the sentence shatters: the Jews "were so blinded by a foolish confidence in the temple... that they thought that God was bound to them," looking on it "as their invincible fortress, as if they dwelt in the bosom of God." But Christ "maintains that it is in vain for them to boast of the presence of God, whom they had driven away by their crimes, and, by calling it their house, he indirectly intimates to them that it is no longer God's." It was "a dreadful vengeance of God, that the place which Himself had so magnificently adorned was not only forsaken... but consigned to the lowest infamy." He turns it against Rome's pretensions to an indefectible holy place.

Wesley sums it: "The temple, which is now your house, not God's: is left unto you — Our Lord spake this as he was going out of it for the last time: Desolate — Forsaken of God and his Christ, and sentenced to utter destruction." The witnesses agree: the divine presence that alone made the house holy was withdrawing with Christ, and what remained was an empty shell appointed to destruction.
39:
"For I say unto you, Ye shall not see me henceforth, till ye shall say, Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord." The witnesses read this closing word as both a sentence of abandonment and a far-off promise of restoration. Gill: they "shall not see me henceforth" from the time of his crucifixion — "directly, and ever after, destitute of his presence" — "till the time comes that the fulness of the Gentiles shall be brought in, and all Israel shall be saved, the Jews shall be converted, and... cheerfully say these words to Christ, who will then appear in his glory."

Barnes reads it of mercy withdrawn and at last embraced: "The day of your mercy is gone by... You will not see me as a merciful Saviour... till you have borne these heavy judgments... until you would be glad to hail a deliverer, and say, Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord" (Romans 11:25-32). Clarke and Ellicott concur that it points to a future conversion: Clarke, "till after the fulness of the Gentiles is brought in... then will ye rejoice, and bless... him that cometh in the name of the Lord" (Romans 11:26-27); Ellicott sees "a reference to the fact that the words from Psalms 118:26 had been uttered by the crowd but a few days before on His solemn entry," and concludes "our Lord points to the second Advent, and to the welcome that will then be given Him by all the true Israel of God. For that generation, and for the outward Israel as such, the abandonment was final."

Barnes closes the chapter with reflections that gather the whole: "Jesus pities dying sinners... He would gather them to him, if they would come... The reason why the wicked are not saved is in their obstinacy. They choose not to be saved." Clarke surveys the eight woes once more, and dwells on the wonder that Christ, "with all the authority of a lawgiver and judge," shows "at the same time the tenderness and compassion of a friend and a father: he beholds their awful state — his eye affects his heart, and he weeps over them." He rejects the notion that Christ "could have fixed their reprobation from all eternity by any necessitating decree, and yet weep over the unavoidable consequences."

The witnesses agree: the chapter that began in woe ends in a deferred hope. Christ leaves the temple and the city to desolation, yet not for ever; the day will come when Israel, long blinded, will at last bless the One they rejected, and welcome him with the very words the crowd had cried at his entry. The abandonment is real, but the door of final mercy is not shut against the whole future of the people.
